walkthrough.title: Check Your Player Count
walkthrough.text: Before anything else, count how many players you have. The Night Cage plays one to five, but the tile mix changes depending on the number of prisoners. Grab the matching Tile Discards reference card — one side says four prisoners, the other says five. This card tells you exactly which tiles and how many of each go into the deck. For four prisoners: 4 Start Tiles, 4 Gates, 6 Keys, 10 Straights, 12 Crossroads, 30 T-Bends, and 12 Wax Eaters. For five prisoners: 5 Start Tiles, 4 Gates, 7 Keys, 10 Straights, 12 Crossroads, 30 T-Bends, and 10 Wax Eaters.
walkthrough.image_caption: Tile Discards reference card for 4 Prisoners — shows all tile types and counts

walkthrough.title: Remove Unused Tiles
walkthrough.text: Now here is where it gets important. Look at your reference card and pull out any tile types that should NOT be in the deck for your player count. For a standard first game, remove ALL advanced tiles — that means Keepers, Pit Fiends, Pathless, and Omens. Set those aside in the box. Then adjust the remaining tiles to match the counts on your reference card. Any extras go back in the box. Getting this right matters — too many or too few tiles changes the game's difficulty significantly.
walkthrough.image_caption: Tile Discards reference card for 5 Prisoners — different tile counts for the larger group

walkthrough.title: Shuffle and Load the Tile Tower
walkthrough.text: Take all the tiles you kept and give them a thorough shuffle face-down. Do not peek at them. Now grab the tile tower — that is the tall vertical card holder with the gorgeous candle-drip artwork on the sides. Stack all the shuffled tiles face-down into the tower. They should fit snugly in a vertical stack. This tower is the heart of the game. Every time you explore, you will draw the top tile from here. When it runs empty, the final countdown begins.
walkthrough.image_caption: Tile tower loaded with the shuffled tile deck

walkthrough.title: Pick Your Prisoner
walkthrough.text: Each player picks a colored Prisoner meeple. There are five to choose from — red, blue, green, yellow, and purple. These are the hooded figures that represent you wandering through the dark. Grab whichever color speaks to you. You are a prisoner trapped in an ever-shifting maze of darkness. All you have is a candle. Choose wisely — just kidding, the color is purely cosmetic. But you will get attached to your little hooded figure.
walkthrough.image_caption: Five colored Prisoner meeples — red, blue, green, yellow, and purple

walkthrough.title: Take a Player Reference Card
walkthrough.text: Each player takes one of the double-sided Player Reference cards. Start with the Normal side facing up — that is the side with the green candle art that says YOUR TURN: MOVE ONE SPACE or STAY. The other side is the Lights Out side, which you will flip to if you ever end up surrounded by darkness. Keep this card in front of you. It is your lifeline for remembering what you can do on your turn and what Nerve lets you do.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player reference card — Normal side showing Move or Stay options and Nerve powers

walkthrough.title: Deal Start Tiles
walkthrough.text: Now pull out the Start Tiles from the deck — you already counted them when checking the reference card. Deal one Start Tile to each player. Place your Start Tile face up on the table in front of you. Spread the Start Tiles apart from each other with plenty of space between them. You begin the game separated, alone in the dark. The empty space between your tiles? That is the void. You will need to explore to fill it in and find each other.
walkthrough.image_caption: Prisoner token — dark disc showing a hooded figure with a candle

walkthrough.title: Place Prisoners on Start Tiles
walkthrough.text: Put your Prisoner meeple on your Start Tile. This is where you wake up. Alone. In the dark. Your candle illuminates the tiles immediately around you — but right now, there is nothing around you. Just void. On your first turn, when you move off your Start Tile, you will draw tiles from the tower to fill in the spaces your candle reveals. The maze is about to take shape.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Monster Supply
walkthrough.text: Place the Wax Eater miniatures near the tile tower where everyone can reach them. These are the creepy gray skeletal creatures with spines and claws. They are the monsters hunting you in the dark. You will place one on the board whenever a Wax Eater tile is revealed. Also set out the Wax Eater tokens — the dark triangular tokens with the swirling flame artwork. These track Wax Eater movement.
walkthrough.image_caption: Wax Eater miniatures — gray skeletal spine creatures that hunt prisoners

walkthrough.title: Everyone Starts at Zero Nerve
walkthrough.text: Nerve is your emergency currency. Everyone starts with zero. The maximum is two. You gain Nerve by choosing to Stay on your turn instead of moving. Nerve lets you do powerful things — move again, block a monster attack, charge through a creature, or sustain a tile during the endgame. You can track it mentally, with coins, dice, or any small tokens. Two is the cap. You will never have more than two, but spending them at the right moment is everything.
walkthrough.image_caption: Lights Out side of the reference card — shows all Nerve spending options

walkthrough.title: Choose First Player and Begin
walkthrough.text: Pick someone to go first — youngest player, last person to be afraid of the dark, or just pick randomly. Play goes clockwise. And that is it. Setup is done. You are alone in the dark with a flickering candle, somewhere in an impossible maze that rebuilds itself around you. The only way out is together. Find the keys. Find the gates. Find each other. Ready? Take your first step into the dark.

bullets: Grab the Tile Discards reference card matching your player count (4 or 5)
4 Prisoners: 4 Start, 4 Gates, 6 Keys, 10 Straights, 12 Crossroads, 30 T-Bends, 12 Wax Eaters
5 Prisoners: 5 Start, 4 Gates, 7 Keys, 10 Straights, 12 Crossroads, 30 T-Bends, 10 Wax Eaters

bullets: Remove ALL advanced tiles for first game (Keepers, Pit Fiends, Pathless, Omens)
Adjust tile counts to match your reference card
Extras go back in the box

bullets: Shuffle all remaining tiles face-down — do not peek
Stack them into the tile tower (vertical card holder)

summary.title: Pick Your Prisoner

Color is cosmetic only

summary.title: Take a Player Reference Card
bullets: Each player takes 1 double-sided reference card
Start with Normal side face up (Move or Stay)
Flip to Lights Out side when surrounded by darkness

summary.title: Deal Start Tiles
bullets: Deal 1 Start Tile to each player, place face up
Spread them apart — prisoners begin separated

summary.title: Place Prisoners on Start Tiles
bullets: Place your meeple on your Start Tile
No adjacent tiles yet — the maze builds as you explore

summary.title: Set Up the Monster Supply
bullets: Place Wax Eater miniatures and tokens within reach

summary.title: Everyone Starts at Zero Nerve

Track with coins, dice, or memory
Gained by Staying; spent for special actions

summary.title: Choose First Player and Begin
bullets: Pick starting player by any method
Play proceeds clockwise

walkthrough.title: The Core Loop
walkthrough.text: You wake up in complete darkness. All you have is a candle — and it won't last forever. Here is what every turn looks like: you either Move one space or Stay where you are. That is it. One action. But that simple choice ripples outward in terrifying ways. When you move, your candle reveals new tiles around you. When you leave, the darkness swallows those tiles back up. The maze is alive, always shifting, and it is not on your side.
walkthrough.image_caption: Player reference card — Move One Space or Stay

walkthrough.title: Moving
walkthrough.text: To move, step your Prisoner meeple one space to any orthogonally adjacent tile — up, down, left, or right. Not diagonal. If the space you move to is empty, draw the top tile from the tower and place it there. The tile's paths must connect to where you came from. After you move, look at all four spaces around your new position. Any empty space adjacent to you gets a new tile drawn from the tower and placed face up. Your candle is illuminating the area around you.

walkthrough.title: The Darkness Returns
walkthrough.text: As you move through the maze, your flickering light reveals the path around you. But here is the thing — when you leave, the darkness swallows those tiles back up. After moving and revealing new tiles, check every tile on the board. If a tile is NOT adjacent to ANY prisoner's current position, it goes dark. Remove it from the board and place it at the bottom of the tile tower. The maze literally disappears behind you. This is why staying together matters. Two prisoners standing near each other keep more tiles illuminated.
walkthrough.image_caption: A path tile — T-Bend connecting two corridors of the maze

walkthrough.title: Staying
walkthrough.text: Instead of moving, you can choose to Stay. When you Stay, pick one tile adjacent to your position and discard it to the bottom of the tower. Then gain one Nerve. The maximum Nerve is two — you cannot go above that. Staying is your way of banking emergency resources. But it costs you a tile and a turn, so use it wisely. Important: you CANNOT Stay if you are in Lights Out status. When the darkness has closed in completely, your only option is to run.

walkthrough.title: Tile Types
walkthrough.text: The maze is made of several tile types. Straights connect two opposite sides. T-Bends connect three sides. Crossroads connect all four sides. These are your basic path tiles — they form the corridors of the maze. Key tiles look special and contain a key. Gate tiles are your escape route. Start Tiles are where each prisoner began. When you draw a tile from the tower, place it so its paths connect logically to the existing maze.
walkthrough.image_caption: Path tiles in storage — the maze waiting to be built

walkthrough.title: Finding Keys
walkthrough.text: Scattered through the tile deck are Key tiles. When you move onto a Key tile, you pick up the key — it is yours now. Every prisoner needs a key to escape. You cannot share keys or trade them. Once you have your key, your next goal is finding a Gate. Keys are the first half of your escape plan. There are just enough keys in the deck for every prisoner to get one, but they could be anywhere in the tower.

walkthrough.title: Gates and Escaping
walkthrough.text: Gate tiles are your way out. But here is the catch — Gates only work when ALL prisoners are holding a Key. Until everyone has a key, Gates are just another tile. Once the last prisoner picks up their key, the race is on. Every prisoner must move to a Gate tile. When all prisoners are on Gates with Keys, everyone escapes together. You win as a group or you die as a group. No one gets left behind.

walkthrough.title: Wax Eaters
walkthrough.text: When a Wax Eater tile is drawn from the tower and placed on the board, put a Wax Eater miniature on it. These skeletal, spine-covered creatures are drawn to your light. At the end of each turn, Wax Eaters move one space toward the nearest prisoner. They follow paths — they do not teleport. When a Wax Eater reaches a prisoner, it attacks. The attack discards three tiles adjacent to the attacked prisoner. The darkness literally closes in around you. This can cascade — losing tiles might leave you in Lights Out.
walkthrough.image_caption: Wax Eater standee — dark disc with a cloaked skeletal figure

walkthrough.title: Lights Out
walkthrough.text: If you ever have zero illuminated tiles adjacent to your position, you are in total darkness. Flip your reference card to the Lights Out side. When you are Lights Out, everything changes. You MUST move — you cannot Stay. You are too panicked to rest. You can still spend Nerve, but your options are restricted. Getting out of Lights Out means moving to a space where new tiles can be drawn around you, reconnecting to the maze. Lights Out is terrifying, but survivable — if you keep your head.
walkthrough.image_caption: Lights Out reference card — restricted actions in total darkness

walkthrough.title: Nerve — Your Emergency Currency
walkthrough.text: Nerve is the most precious resource in The Night Cage. You gain it by Staying (one Nerve per Stay, max two). You spend one Nerve to do any of these: Move Again — take a second move this turn. Stay while Lights Out — override the panic and rest anyway. Block — reduce a monster attack from three discards to two. Charge — move onto AND through a monster tile, taking the attack but scrambling past. Sustain — during Final Flickers, prevent a tile from being removed. You cannot Sustain if you gained Nerve this turn.
walkthrough.image_caption: Wax Eater tokens — triangular tokens with flame art used for tracking

walkthrough.title: Block
walkthrough.text: When a Wax Eater attacks you and would discard three tiles around you, you can spend one Nerve to Block. Blocking reduces the attack — you only discard two tiles instead of three. That one saved tile could be the difference between staying connected to the maze and falling into Lights Out. Block is a defensive reaction, not something you do on your turn. You use it the instant an attack hits.

walkthrough.title: Charge
walkthrough.text: Sometimes a Wax Eater is blocking the only path forward. Spending one Nerve to Charge lets you move onto a monster tile and then immediately move through it to a tile on the other side. You take the full attack — three tiles discarded around you — but you end up past the monster. It is desperate and costly, but sometimes the only way through. Think of it as putting your head down and sprinting through the nightmare.

walkthrough.title: Final Flickers
walkthrough.text: When the tile tower runs empty, the endgame begins. No more new tiles will be drawn. From now on, after each player's turn, one illuminated tile adjacent to them is removed. The maze is collapsing. You can spend one Nerve to Sustain — prevent that tile from being removed this turn. But you cannot Sustain if you gained Nerve this turn, so you cannot Stay and Sustain in the same turn. During Final Flickers, every single move counts. The path to the Gates is shrinking behind you.

walkthrough.title: Winning Together
walkthrough.text: To win, every prisoner must be holding a Key and standing on a Gate tile. When the last prisoner reaches a Gate with their Key, the cage opens and everyone escapes. You all win. There is no scoring, no ranking, no individual victory. You survive together or you perish together in the dark. The difficulty scales with player count — more prisoners means more keys to find and more coordination required.

walkthrough.title: Losing Together
walkthrough.text: You lose if any prisoner becomes permanently trapped — no legal moves, no Nerve to escape, no hope. You also lose if the tile tower empties and players cannot sustain enough tiles to reach Gates before the maze collapses entirely. When the darkness wins, it wins completely. Everyone loses. There is no partial credit in The Night Cage. The maze does not care how close you got.

The maze shifts constantly around you

No scoring — cooperative pass/fail

summary.title: Losing Together

Lose if tower empties and players cannot reach Gates before maze collapses

walkthrough.title: Stay Together
walkthrough.text: This is rule number one, and it overrides almost everything else. When two prisoners are adjacent or near each other, their candles overlap, keeping more tiles illuminated. Every tile that stays lit is one more option, one more escape route, one more turn of breathing room. When prisoners drift apart, the darkness between them devours those tiles. A solo prisoner bleeds tiles every turn. Two prisoners together preserve the maze. Move toward each other as your first priority.

walkthrough.title: Nerve Management
walkthrough.text: Nerve is capped at two, so do not obsess over hoarding it — but do not waste it either. The ideal rhythm is: Stay to bank one Nerve when you are in a safe position, then spend it when a crisis hits. A common mistake is burning Nerve on convenience — using Move Again just to explore faster when there is no urgency. Save Nerve for genuine emergencies: Blocking an attack that would push you into Lights Out, Charging through a Wax Eater blocking the only path to a Key, or Sustaining during Final Flickers.

walkthrough.title: Path Toward Keys Early
walkthrough.text: Keys are randomly shuffled into the tower, so you cannot guarantee when they appear. But you can increase your odds by exploring purposefully rather than wandering. Move in a consistent direction — spreading outward from your Start Tile in a line or arc rather than doubling back. Every new tile you reveal is a chance to find a Key. The sooner everyone has their Key, the sooner you can focus on reaching Gates. Do not wait until Final Flickers to start looking.

walkthrough.title: Communicate Everything
walkthrough.text: The Night Cage is a cooperative game, and communication is your most powerful tool. Call out what tiles you see around you. Announce when you spot a Key or Gate. Warn teammates when a Wax Eater is heading their direction. Coordinate movement so you do not accidentally orphan tiles between you. A simple 'I am moving east this turn' lets your partner plan their next move to keep the shared tiles alive.

walkthrough.title: Wax Eater Avoidance vs Engagement
walkthrough.text: When a Wax Eater appears, you have two philosophies. Avoidance: move away, let it chase you, try to outrun it. This works if you have space and the Wax Eater is not between you and your objective. Engagement: spend Nerve to Charge through it, take the three-tile hit, and clear the path. This is better when the Wax Eater is blocking the route to a Key or Gate and rerouting would cost multiple turns. Generally, avoid early and engage late. In Final Flickers, you do not have turns to waste on detours.

walkthrough.title: The Staying Calculus
walkthrough.text: Staying costs a tile and a turn but gives you one Nerve. When is it worth it? Stay when you are in a safe position with multiple adjacent tiles, no immediate threats, and your Nerve is at zero or one. Do NOT Stay when you are near a Wax Eater (you are giving it a turn to close in), when you have two Nerve already (it is wasted), or when time pressure demands forward progress. The ideal Stay is from a Crossroads tile — four connections mean you have options afterward.

walkthrough.title: Final Flickers Planning
walkthrough.text: When the tower gets low — maybe ten or fifteen tiles left — start planning your endgame. Ask yourselves: does everyone have a Key? Where are the Gates? How many turns will it take each prisoner to reach one? Sustain can buy you time, but only if you have Nerve saved and did not gain Nerve that turn. The golden scenario is: all prisoners have Keys, a Gate or two is visible, and everyone is within two or three moves of one. If you are not in that position when the tower empties, start making sacrificial moves to create a path.

walkthrough.title: Tile Tower Awareness
walkthrough.text: Keep a rough mental count of how many tiles are left in the tower. Every tile drawn (by moving) and every tile returned (by darkness or Staying) changes the count. When the tower gets thin, every move draws you closer to Final Flickers. This awareness should influence your decisions — if the tower is flush, explore freely. If it is getting thin, tighten up your movement, stop wandering, and converge on known Gates.

walkthrough.title: Sustain Timing
walkthrough.text: During Final Flickers, Sustain is your most important Nerve ability. It prevents one tile from being removed after your turn. But the restriction — you cannot Sustain if you gained Nerve this turn — means you cannot Stay and Sustain on the same turn. Plan ahead: if you know Final Flickers is one or two turns away, bank Nerve NOW so you have it when the flickers start. Two prisoners each with two Nerve can Sustain four times total. That could be enough.

walkthrough.title: Sacrificial Movement
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the best move for the group is bad for you individually. Moving away from the group to draw a Wax Eater away from a teammate carrying a Key. Staying on a tile that will be consumed by darkness to buy another prisoner one more turn to reach a Gate. Charging through a monster so the path behind you is clear for a slower teammate. The Night Cage rewards selfless play. The question is never 'how do I survive?' — it is 'how do WE survive?'

entries.definition: A path tile connecting two opposite sides, forming a straight corridor. One of the three basic tile types. 10 in the deck for both 4-player and 5-player games.
A path tile connecting three sides, forming a T-shaped intersection. The most common tile type in the deck. 30 in the deck for both 4-player and 5-player games.
A path tile connecting all four sides, forming a full intersection. 12 in the deck for both 4-player and 5-player games. Crossroads are the most flexible tile — they connect in every direction.

A special tile containing a key. When a prisoner moves onto a Key tile, they pick up the key. Each prisoner needs one Key to escape through a Gate. 6 Keys for 4 players, 7 Keys for 5 players. Keys cannot be shared or traded between prisoners.
The escape tiles. Gates only activate when ALL prisoners are holding a Key. Once activated, each prisoner must move onto a Gate tile to complete the escape. 4 Gate tiles in all player counts.

Each prisoner begins the game on a Start Tile, placed face up and spread apart on the table. Start Tiles function as normal path tiles after the game begins. 4 Start Tiles for 4 players, 5 for 5 players.

When revealed from the tower, a Wax Eater miniature is placed on this tile. The creature then moves toward prisoners and attacks. 12 Wax Eaters for 4 players (4 in Advanced Mode), 10 for 5 players (2 in Advanced Mode).

At the end of each turn, each Wax Eater on the board moves one space along connected paths toward the nearest prisoner. Wax Eaters follow path connections — they cannot teleport or cross empty spaces.

When a Wax Eater reaches a prisoner's tile, it attacks. The attack forces the targeted prisoner to discard 3 tiles adjacent to their position. This can cascade into Lights Out if all surrounding tiles are consumed. Can be reduced to 2 discards with Block.

The primary action on your turn. Move your Prisoner meeple one space orthogonally (up, down, left, or right — not diagonal) to an adjacent tile. If moving to an empty space, draw and place a tile from the tower. After moving, reveal tiles for all empty adjacent spaces and remove unlit tiles.

The alternative to moving. Discard one tile adjacent to your position (your choice) to the bottom of the tower, then gain 1 Nerve (max 2). You cannot Stay if you are Lights Out. Staying costs a tile and a turn but banks emergency resources.
Spend 1 Nerve to take a second move this turn. Useful for closing distance to a Key, Gate, or teammate. Available in both Normal and Lights Out status.

Spend 1 Nerve to Stay even though you are in Lights Out status. Normally, Lights Out forces you to move. This overrides that restriction. You still discard a tile and gain 1 Nerve as normal (net zero Nerve since you spent 1 and gained 1). Gain no Nerve if already at max.

Spend 1 Nerve when a Wax Eater attacks you. Instead of discarding 3 adjacent tiles, you only discard 2. This is a reaction — used the instant an attack hits, not on your turn. The saved tile can prevent a cascade into Lights Out.

Spend 1 Nerve to move onto a Monster tile and immediately move through it to a connected tile on the other side. You take the full attack (3 tile discards) but end up past the monster. Desperate but effective when a Wax Eater blocks the only viable path.

Available only during Final Flickers. Spend 1 Nerve to prevent one illuminated tile from being removed after your turn. Critical restriction: you cannot Sustain if you gained Nerve this turn. This means you cannot Stay and Sustain on the same turn. Plan your Nerve banking before Final Flickers begins.

The emergency resource in The Night Cage. Gained by Staying (1 per Stay). Maximum of 2 Nerve per prisoner. Spent for special actions: Move Again, Stay (Lights Out), Block, Charge, and Sustain. Cannot exceed 2 regardless of source.

A status triggered when a prisoner has zero illuminated tiles adjacent to their position — they are surrounded by void. Flip the reference card to the Lights Out side. You MUST move (cannot Stay). Special Nerve options are available but restricted. Escape by moving to a space where new tiles can be drawn.

The endgame phase that begins when the tile tower is completely empty. No new tiles are drawn when moving to empty spaces. After each player's turn, one illuminated tile adjacent to them is removed (unless they Sustain). The maze rapidly collapses. Players must reach Gates before all tiles vanish.

Any tile that is adjacent to at least one prisoner. Illuminated tiles remain on the board. A tile that is not adjacent to any prisoner is considered dark and is removed to the bottom of the tower. Keeping tiles illuminated requires prisoners to stay near each other.

The vertical card holder where all shuffled tiles are stored face-down. Tiles are drawn from the top when prisoners explore. Removed tiles go to the bottom. When the tower empties, Final Flickers begins. The tower is the game's timer — every tile drawn brings the end closer.

Each player controls one Prisoner — a hooded figure trapped in the maze with only a candle. Prisoners are represented by colored wooden meeples (red, blue, green, yellow, purple) and matching tokens. All prisoners must escape together for the group to win.

Advanced mode tiles that replace Keys. Keepers have different pickup mechanics and add complexity to the escape objective. When using Keepers, remove all Key tiles from the deck and add Keeper tiles instead. Replaces Keys entirely. 6 Keepers for 4 players, 7 for 5 players.

Advanced mode monster tiles. Pit Fiends add a second type of creature to the maze alongside Wax Eaters. They have different movement and attack patterns. 2 Pit Fiend tiles are added to the deck in Advanced Mode.
Boss-level advanced tiles. Pathless tiles create areas of the maze with no path connections — dead zones that prisoners must navigate around. 2 Pathless tiles available in the full advanced deck.

Boss-level advanced tiles. Omens introduce special event-like effects when revealed from the tower. They add unpredictability and tension to the advanced game. 2 Omen tiles available in the full advanced deck.

Standard mode for 4 prisoners: 4 Start Tiles, 4 Gates, 6 Keys, 10 Straights, 12 Crossroads, 30 T-Bends, 12 Wax Eaters. Advanced additions: 6 Keepers (replace Keys), 2 Pit Fiends, 2 Pathless, 2 Omens, 4 Wax Eaters (in Advanced Mode).

Standard mode for 5 prisoners: 5 Start Tiles, 4 Gates, 7 Keys, 10 Straights, 12 Crossroads, 30 T-Bends, 10 Wax Eaters. Advanced additions: 7 Keepers (replace Keys), 2 Pit Fiends, 2 Pathless, 2 Omens, 2 Wax Eaters (in Advanced Mode).

Double-sided card given to each player. Normal side shows: YOUR TURN — Move One Space or Stay, plus all Nerve spending options. Lights Out side shows: YOUR TURN — Move One Space (you may not Stay), plus restricted Nerve spending options. Flip between sides as your status changes.
